questions.question: What are the four essential life processes required for maintaining life?
questions.explanation: These processes work together to provide energy, move materials within the body, and remove toxic waste products to maintain a living state.

questions.question: What is the role of 'Salivary Amylase' in the digestion process?
questions.correct_answer: It breaks down complex starch into simple sugars (maltose)
questions.explanation: Digestion begins in the mouth where this enzyme, secreted by salivary glands, starts the chemical breakdown of carbohydrates.

questions.question: Differentiate between Autotrophic and Heterotrophic nutrition.
questions.correct_answer: Autotrophs make their own food; Heterotrophs depend on others
questions.explanation: Autotrophs (like plants) use simple inorganic materials ($CO_2$, water) to synthesize food. Heterotrophs (like animals/fungi) consume complex organic substances.

questions.question: What happens to the pyruvate molecule during anaerobic respiration in our muscles?
questions.correct_answer: It is converted into Lactic Acid and energy
questions.explanation: During sudden physical activity, oxygen demand exceeds supply. Pyruvate is converted to lactic acid, which can cause muscle cramps.

questions.question: Why is 'Double Circulation' necessary in humans?
questions.correct_answer: To prevent mixing of oxygenated and deoxygenated blood
questions.explanation: This ensures an efficient supply of oxygen to the body, which is essential for warm-blooded animals to maintain constant body temperature.

questions.question: What is the function of 'Villi' in the small intestine?
questions.correct_answer: To increase the surface area for rapid absorption of digested food
questions.explanation: Villi are finger-like projections richly supplied with blood vessels that take absorbed food to every cell of the body.

questions.question: Explain the role of the 'Glomerulus' in the human excretory system.
questions.correct_answer: It acts as a high-pressure filter to remove nitrogenous waste from blood
questions.explanation: It is a cluster of thin-walled capillaries in the Bowman's capsule where ultrafiltration of blood takes place.

questions.question: How do guard cells regulate the opening and closing of stomatal pores?
questions.correct_answer: By swelling (opening) or shrinking (closing) through water absorption
questions.explanation: When water flows into them, they swell and become curved, opening the pore. When they lose water, they shrink and the pore closes.

questions.question: What is 'Translocation' in plants?
questions.correct_answer: The transport of soluble products of photosynthesis (food) via Phloem
questions.explanation: Unlike water transport, translocation requires energy in the form of ATP to move food from leaves to other parts.

questions.question: Identify the site of 'Aerobic Respiration' in a cell.
questions.explanation: The breakdown of pyruvate using oxygen to release energy, $CO_2$, and water happens inside the mitochondria.

notes.title: ATP (Adenosine Triphosphate)
notes.content: Known as the 'Energy Currency' of the cell; used to fuel various metabolic activities.

notes.title: Bile Juice
notes.content: Secreted by the liver; it makes the medium alkaline for pancreatic enzymes and emulsifies fats.

notes.title: Platelets
notes.content: Specialized blood cells responsible for clotting blood at the site of injury to prevent blood loss.

facts.fact: The length of the small intestine differs in animals; herbivores have longer ones to digest cellulose.
Fishes have a two-chambered heart and blood is oxygenated in the gills.
Transpiration pull is the major driving force for water movement in tall trees during the day.
